commit_message:
[rom_ctrl] Squash ROM bus responses on an internal error

There are a few awkward corner cases surrounding the mux, where an
injected fault can cause a response to come out as well as triggering
an alert. We've got some extra layers of safety here (e.g. ensuring
that the response data will have malformed integrity), but it's not
hard to explicitly squash things as well.

The result will be that the bus transaction hangs (and the chip will
eventually watchdog).

Signed-off-by: Rupert Swarbrick <rswarbrick@lowrisc.org>
pull_title: [rom_ctrl] Squash ROM bus responses on an internal error
pull_body:
There are a few awkward corner cases surrounding the mux, where an
injected fault can cause a response to come out as well as triggering
an alert. We've got some extra layers of safety here (e.g. ensuring
that the response data will have malformed integrity), but it's not
hard to explicitly squash things as well.

The result will be that the bus transaction hangs (and the chip will
eventually watchdog).
Issue title: [rom_ctrl,rtl] Fix a bug in the mux with invalid signals
Issue Body: This fixes a bug discovered by @prajwalaputtappa with her signal corruption  tests. Suppose you corrupt the `mubi4_t` that appears here as `sel_bus_i`  some time after the ROM check has completed.    With the previous code, we sent `chk_req_i` (constant 1) to the ROM and  then reflected it back as `bus_rvalid_o` on the cycle after the glitch  went away. This, in turn, caused an assertion to go off in the TL  converter. In the real design, we might have even manufactured a stray  bus response. Oops!    This commit makes sure that we only make a request if `sel_q` is one of  the allowed `mubi4_t` values.
Comments: rswarbrick: Hmm, it turns out that there is a second scenario that would still let an unexpected response out (by somehow switching the mubi select to the opposite valid signal). That should be difficult to trigger but, since we've got some countermeasures in place to handle it, we should probably be consistent. I'm going to close this and open a new PR with a more general fix. rswarbrick: There's a better fix (hopefully) at #10444.
